schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch frame the chronicler's design (Judah's history under the LORD's law); Ellicott marks the old accusative 'Shechem' and the plural 'were come' as the chronicler's independent hand over his 1 Kings source.
Henry draws hard lessons: the wisest cannot please all, ungrateful spirits complain without cause, and Rehoboam wore his father's crown but wanted his father's brains (cf. Job 31:13).
Clarke summarizes the whole chapter and notes it is almost word for word 1 Kings 12:1-19.

Ellicott reads 'who was in Egypt' as a parenthesis, notes the chronicler drops Kings' 'still' (odennu) because he never told of the flight (1 Kings 11:26-40), and that 'dwelt'/'returned' share the same consonants; Keil carries the narrative of the recalled exile forward.
Ellicott: they summoned Jeroboam to the assembly (cf. 1 Kings 12:20); the chronicler shortens Kings' 'assembly of Israel' to 'all Israel' and writes the singular 'came' for Kings' plural.
Ellicott: read 'made hard' and 'lighten'; the chronicler avoids Kings' assonance 'and thou now' (w'attah attah) at the cost of the emphasis on 'thou' (cf. v.10).
Wesley: the yoke was grievous because, when Solomon declined from God, God left him to act impolitically.

Ellicott: literally 'Yet three days, and return'; the imperative 'go ye' has likely dropped out (kept in LXX/Syriac/Arabic); 'the people departed' is singular, contrasting the plural gathering of v.1.

themes: the space of a fatal choice

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'before Solomon' is the later liphne Shelomoh for Kings' eth-pene Shelomoh; 'to return answer to this people' is literally 'to return a word,' the double-accusative preserved at v.9.

Ellicott: the chronicler's 'be kind' is a free paraphrase of Kings' 'become a servant... and serve them'; 'please them' is 'be propitious, receive graciously' (ratsah, Genesis 33:10) for Kings' 'answer them.'
Wesley: moderate counsels are best; gentleness does what violence cannot; good words cost only a little self-denial yet purchase great things.
Ellicott: in 'that stood before him' the chronicler drops a redundant relative particle the older text carried, one more mark of his habitual compression.

themes: the young men brought up with him

schools.summary: Ellicott: the verse agrees with Kings (1 Kings 12:9) to the letter; where the chronicler does not abridge he copies exactly, evidence of faithful transmission. The king relays the demand of v.4 to his peers.
Clarke cites the Targum's paraphrase of the taunt: 'My weakness shall be stronger than the might of my father.'
Ellicott: 'spake with him' is a mistaken repetition (Kings/LXX 'unto him,' 1 Kings 12:10); 'lighten from upon us'; 'finger' should not be italicised, since qoten means 'little finger.'

themes: the little finger and the loins
the tone that loses a kingdom

schools.summary: Ellicott: literally 'And now, my father... and I, I will add to your yoke'; 'the whips... the scorpions'; the whole saying stands in Kings and recurs almost unchanged at v.14.

Henry's lessons: violent proceedings make bad worse; God fulfils His word through the king's free folly (Ahijah, v.15) without excusing it; dominion is uncertain (v.16); iniquity visited on children, yet the promise to David keeps two tribes (Psalm 89:31-34).
Ellicott: the verse stands 'literatim as Kings,' copied word for word.
Ellicott (1 Kings 12:13): the chronicler writes 'them' for Kings' 'the people,' glosses 'roughly' as 'hardly'/harshly, and names 'king Rehoboam' where Kings instead adds 'that they counselled him.'

Ellicott (1 Kings 12:14): the Targum and many Hebrew MSS read 'I will make heavy,' a copyist's fusion of abi hikhbid into ahbid; all the versions support the received 'my father made your yoke heavy.'

themes: the speech that breaks the kingdom

schools.summary: Clarke gives the Targum's guard on the divine justice: 'For there was an occasion Divinely given' — God supplied the occasion, not the sin.

Ellicott: 'the cause was of God' is 'a turn/turning-point from with God' (nesibbah = Kings' sibbah; cf. sibbath sibboth); it fulfils Ahijah's word (1 Kings 11:29), which the chronicler never records but alludes to (2 Chronicles 9:29).

Ellicott: the chronicler writes 'Now all Israel had seen' (Kings 'And all Israel saw,' 'saw' absent from many MSS); 'man' in 'every man to your tents' is likely spurious, a repetition of Yishai/Jesse (cf. 1 Kings 12:16); 'see to thine own house' = govern Judah alone.
Wesley: violent proceedings make ill worse; many are driven to mischief by being too severely dealt with — a warning against the king's harshness.

themes: What portion have we in David
the secession of the ten tribes

schools.summary: Clarke, following Jarchi: Hadoram was stoned when he tried to collect the tribute Solomon had imposed — the officer of the hated levy sent into a people in revolt.

Ellicott: Hadoram = Kings' Adoram, LXX Adoniram (cf. 1 Kings 4:6); 'the tribute' is 'the levy' of forced labour; 'children of Israel' for Kings' 'all Israel'; 'the royal chariot' of the king's undignified flight.

Clarke: a few soft words would have saved the kingdom and a sea of blood — 'Rehoboam was a fool; and through his folly he lost his kingdom,' a folly repeated by the Stuarts and avoided by the House of Brunswick.
Ellicott: neither compiler altered 'unto this day,' a phrase no longer fitting their own times (cf. 1 Chronicles 5:26) — a fossil proving faithful transmission.

outliers.note: Turns the verse into a political-historical excursus, likening Rehoboam's folly to the Stuarts' loss of England (versus the House of Brunswick) and closing with Virgil, Horace, and 'Amen! Amen!'

themes: a kingdom lost for a little kindness
'unto this day' as a fossil phrase
